episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Repair Culture & Practical Tech
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Repair Culture & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Quesnel Cariboo Observer] B.C. shipyard ready to deliver on new navy submarines and [Williams Lake Tribune] UPDATE: Brunswick Creek wildfire remains out of control as response continues, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Is repair culture and durable tech a viable solution for rural resilience, or merely a lifestyle choice for the already-affluent? Topics include: Premium pricing of durable/repairable tech vs. disposable alternatives, Regional co-purchasing as a volume strategy for rural areas, DIY/open-design solutions as manufacturer-independent repair approaches, Library programs and repair cafés as community knowledge infrastructure, Open-source AI models running on older hardware as repair-culture infrastructure, Provincial procurement policy as a lever to shift manufacturer incentives.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Regional District Library

discussion.central_question: Is repair culture and durable tech a viable solution for rural resilience, or merely a lifestyle choice for the already-affluent?
discussion.riley_position: Repairability isn't inherently expensive—it's a market failure caused by lack of scale and regulatory pressure. Multiple levers exist outside grant cycles: co-purchasing, DIY design, procurement policy, and open-source tools can shift incentives without waiting for manufacturers to voluntarily change.
riley_key_evidence: Fairphone and Framework proved market demand exists; scale is what's missing, not feasibility
Hackaday sailing kit uses off-the-shelf hardware and requires only an afternoon and basic skills
EU right-to-repair rules forced manufacturers to change without relying on market generosity
discussion.casey_position: Repairable tech costs significantly more upfront than disposable alternatives, and knowledge/capacity gaps prevent rural residents from actually using repair solutions. The real structural barriers aren't solved by procurement policy or library programs—they require either funding or someone volunteering unpaid labor.
casey_key_evidence: Jackery power station, rugged smartphone, and flip phone all cost substantially more than disposable versions
Cariboo Regional District (65,000 people) lacks volume to move manufacturer production lines
Most people don't think 'I could build this'—knowledge gap prevents adoption of DIY solutions
discussion.resolution: They agreed to disagree on affordability but converged on concrete, low-cost levers: provincial procurement policy, small-scale library pilots (one session, one branch), and pushing MLA representatives on equipment standards. Casey insisted on pilot testing before claiming scalability; Riley acknowledged the affordability question remains unresolved by any single lever.
topics_covered: Premium pricing of durable/repairable tech vs. disposable alternatives
Regional co-purchasing as a volume strategy for rural areas
DIY/open-design solutions as manufacturer-independent repair approaches
Library programs and repair cafés as community knowledge infrastructure
Open-source AI models running on older hardware as repair-culture infrastructure
Provincial procurement policy as a lever to shift manufacturer incentives
